items.q: Why were 19th-century industrial cities so unhealthy?
items.correct: Rapid population growth outpaced housing, water and sewage; lack of regulation produced overcrowded slums and cholera epidemics until reforms followed Chadwick's 1842 report.
items.misconception: Cities were unhealthy because Victorians did not care about hygiene.
items.random_wrong: Cities were unhealthy because factory smoke poisoned the food.
items.why: Victorians cared deeply but lacked germ theory and assumed disease came from bad air. The real problem was infrastructure, not indifference — leading to landmark public health acts.

items.q: Why did the US lose the Vietnam War?
items.correct: A determined nationalist insurgency, unwinnable counter-insurgency conditions, collapsing political support at home, and a corrupt South Vietnamese ally.
items.misconception: The US lost because the media turned the public against the war.
items.random_wrong: The US lost because it was outnumbered and outgunned militarily.
items.why: The 'media stab in the back' is a popular but contested claim. The US had overwhelming firepower; the deeper problem was political — fighting a nationalist movement with an unpopular client state.

items.q: Did the Battle of Bosworth in 1485 end the Wars of the Roses?
items.correct: It killed Richard III and put Henry Tudor on the throne, but Yorkist threats (Simnel 1487, Warbeck 1490s) continued — the wars only truly ended with Stoke Field in 1487.
items.misconception: Bosworth in 1485 ended the Wars of the Roses immediately.
items.random_wrong: Bosworth was a naval battle fought off the south coast of England.
items.why: Henry VII faced rebellions for over a decade. Students learn 1485 as a tidy endpoint; in fact securing the dynasty took further fighting and ruthless control of pretenders.

items.q: Which 1950 law classified all South Africans by race?
items.why: It's Population Registration Act. “Group Areas Act” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In which year was Nelson Mandela released from prison?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1990. “1976” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In which year were South Africa's first non-racial elections held?
items.why: The real figure is 1994. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
